questions.q: Identify the relationship between artificial intelligence (AI) and machine learning (ML) as defined in this episode.
options: AI and ML are two separate fields that solve different problems
ML is the broad goal and AI is one technique used to achieve it
ML is a subset of AI — every ML system is AI, but not every AI system uses ML
AI is a subset of ML, because all intelligent systems must learn from data
questions.explanation: The line to weld into memory is 'AI is the goal; ML is one way' — ML sits inside AI as a subset. Every machine learning system is an AI system, but not every AI system uses machine learning (a hand-written expert system is AI without ML). Option D reverses the containment, which is the most common error on this dot-point.

questions.q: What is the single dividing line the episode uses to tell artificial intelligence apart from machine learning?
options: Whether the system runs on a robot or on a normal computer
Whether the behaviour came from rules a human wrote, or from patterns learned from data
Whether the system uses if-statements or loops
Whether the system is connected to big data or runs offline
questions.explanation: Learning is the dividing line: if the behaviour came from data, it is machine learning; if it came from a human writing rules, it is at most rule-based AI. Option A repeats the weak 'AI is robots, ML is computers learning' answer the episode says earns nothing, because it names neither the containment relationship nor learning-from-data.

questions.q: Outline why a rule-based ticket classifier (Listing 1) that sorts messages using hand-written keyword rules counts as AI but NOT as machine learning.
options: It counts as ML because it sorts data into categories automatically
It is neither AI nor ML, because it only uses if-statements
It is ML because it improves every time it processes a new ticket
It is a classic expert system (AI) but learns nothing — a human wrote every rule, so it never improves on its own
questions.explanation: A rule-based classifier is a classic expert system: the rules are hand-written, fixed, and transparent, so it qualifies as AI, but it does the same thing forever until a human edits the rules. It is not ML because nothing learned anything — learning from data is the missing ingredient. The trap is the 'it improves every time' option: this system never improves on its own, unlike the learned classifier in Listing 2.

questions.q: A student claims: 'Our app uses AI because it has if-statements that respond to the user.' Evaluate this claim.
options: Justified — any program that responds to user input with conditional logic is AI
Justified — if-statements are a form of machine learning because the program makes decisions
Not justified — if-statements are ordinary conditional logic; a system is only AI when it performs a task that genuinely requires intelligence, and it is not ML because nothing learned from data
Not justified — the app would only be AI if it were written in a specialised AI programming language
questions.explanation: A long if-else chain is just conditional logic that almost every program has; it earns the AI label only when genuinely reasoning, planning, or perceiving (like an expert system). It is certainly not ML because a human wrote every branch and nothing learned from data. Option D invents a non-existent criterion — the language used is irrelevant; what matters is whether the system reasons or learns.

questions.q: Identify the three automation approaches the syllabus names alongside this dot-point, and what each one automates.
options: DevOps (the build-test-deploy pipeline), RPA (an individual task at the user interface), and BPA (an end-to-end business process)
DevOps (databases), RPA (the deployment pipeline), and BPA (a single repetitive task)
CI/CD (testing only), RPA (whole business processes), and BPA (clicking buttons on screen)
DevOps (front-end design), RPA (machine learning models), and BPA (cloud hosting)
questions.explanation: DevOps automates the software delivery pipeline (continuous integration and continuous deployment), RPA automates an individual task by mimicking a user at the interface, and BPA automates and redesigns an entire end-to-end process. Option B swaps RPA and BPA's scope — RPA is task-level, not pipeline-level, and BPA is process-level, not a single task.

questions.q: Outline the defining characteristic of robotic process automation (RPA) using the exam keyword from the episode.
options: RPA redesigns an entire business process by integrating multiple back-end systems
RPA is a Robot doing a Task — rule-driven, task-level automation that mimics a human user at the interface
RPA automates the build-test-deploy pipeline so code is shipped automatically
RPA uses machine learning to make decisions the rules do not cover
questions.explanation: The exam keyword is task: RPA is 'a Robot doing a Task' — a software bot that clicks buttons and reads fields off a screen, mimicking a human at the user interface, often built with low-code tools. Option A describes BPA (redesigning a process), and option D describes how ML enhances RPA — plain RPA is rule-based and cannot make uncovered decisions.

questions.q: Compare robotic process automation (RPA) and business process automation (BPA).
options: RPA and BPA are synonyms — both redesign processes by integrating multiple systems
RPA is process-level and strategic, while BPA is a quick task-level bot working at the surface
RPA integrates deeply with back-end systems, while BPA only clicks buttons on the front end

Both reduce manual effort, but RPA automates a single task by mimicking a user at the interface and leaves the process unchanged, whereas BPA orchestrates and redesigns an entire end-to-end process with deep system integration and human approvals
questions.explanation: A full-mark comparison states the similarity (both reduce manual effort) then contrasts on scope and depth: RPA is task-level and surface-level via the user interface and keeps the process as-is, while BPA is process-level, deeply integrated, and redesigns the workflow with approvals and an audit trail. The 'synonyms' option repeats the most common error, and the other two distractors swap the two definitions.

questions.q: A finance team manually copies figures from scanned invoices into the accounting system one at a time, and that single repetitive task is the only thing they want sped up. Recommend the most appropriate automation approach and the keyword that decides it.
options: BPA, because copying invoice data touches the accounting system
RPA, because the keyword is task — they want one repetitive task automated by mimicking the user, without redesigning the process
DevOps, because it automates the build-test-deploy pipeline for the finance software
BPA, because the keyword is process — any work involving invoices is a full business process
questions.explanation: The tell is scope: one task, mimic the user, keep the process as-is — that is RPA every time, decided by the keyword task. It only flips to BPA if they redesign the whole accounts-payable process across multiple systems with manager approvals. DevOps is irrelevant here because it automates software delivery, not business data entry.

questions.q: Explain how machine learning enhances a rule-based RPA invoice-processing workflow that breaks whenever the invoice layout changes.
options: ML replaces the RPA bot entirely, so no rule-based steps remain in the workflow
ML makes the bot click buttons faster, so more invoices are processed per minute

ML adds intelligent decision-making — a classifier reads invoices of varying layouts and an anomaly detector flags unusual amounts — while RPA still moves the data, so the workflow handles unstructured input and exceptions that rules alone cannot
ML lets the bot read from fixed screen positions more accurately so the layout no longer matters
questions.explanation: A strong answer follows limitation, capability, effect: plain RPA only handles structured input read from fixed positions, ML adds judgement (classifying documents of varying layout, extracting fields, detecting anomalies), and the effect is a higher proportion of cases handled without human intervention. Automation provides the hands; ML provides the eyes and judgement. Option B is the empty 'makes it better and faster' claim that names no mechanism and scores nothing.

questions.q: Distinguish DevOps from RPA and BPA in terms of what each approach is aimed at.
options: DevOps automates business data entry, while RPA and BPA automate the software pipeline
DevOps is aimed inward at how the engineering team builds, tests, and deploys software, whereas RPA and BPA are aimed at automating business work — a task and an end-to-end process respectively
DevOps, RPA, and BPA all automate the same build-test-deploy pipeline using machine learning
DevOps automates an end-to-end business process with approvals, while BPA automates only a single task
questions.explanation: DevOps is automation aimed inward — the continuous integration and continuous deployment pipeline that builds, tests, and deploys software for the engineering team. RPA and BPA instead automate business operations: RPA a single task by mimicking a user, and BPA an orchestrated end-to-end process. Option A reverses DevOps and the business approaches, and option D confuses DevOps with BPA's approval-bearing workflow.
